source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 5.6 install command (pp. 78-79)

questions.q: Despite its name, what does the install command actually do?
options: Copies files and creates directories while managing permissions, ownership and backups
Installs software packages from a repository
questions.why: install is NOT for packages; it combines cp and mkdir with chmod/chown and backup features in one command.

questions.q: Create the directory ~/samples using the install command.
questions.why: -d treats its arguments as directory names to create (like mkdir).

questions.q: Which install options set the permissions, and the ownership, of the destination?
options: -m sets permissions; -o and -g set the owner and group
questions.why: -m sets the mode (permissions), -o the owner and -g the group; sudo is needed to change ownership.

questions.q: What does the -t option of install do?
options: Copies all the source arguments INTO the given target directory
Tests the copy without performing it
questions.why: -t names the target directory that the source files are copied into (-p is the one that preserves timestamps).

questions.q: With 'install -b', how is the backup of an existing destination file named by default?
options: The original name with a tilde (~) appended, e.g. sample.txt~
questions.why: -b backs up the existing destination by appending a ~ to its name; -S lets you choose a different suffix.

questions.q: Copy src/sample.txt into ~/samples/ (creating leading dirs, verbose) and back up any existing file using the suffix .bak instead of ~.
questions.why: -b enables the backup, -S sets its suffix to .bak; -D creates leading components, -t is the target directory, -v is verbose.

questions.q: Which install option preserves the timestamps of the source files on the copy?
questions.why: -p keeps the source files' timestamps (compare with cp -p).
